coredata.dc:title: Early detection of internal short circuit faults in lithium-ion battery packs using dynamic time warping and Gaussian mixture clustering
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Zuo, Qi
Zhang, Meng
Fu, Ke
Du, Xiaogang
Liu, Zhuang
Lyu, Chao
coredata.dc:description: Internal short circuit (ISC) faults are a primary trigger for thermal runaway in energy storage lithium-ion battery systems. Timely detection of ISC faults at their early stage can effectively prevent severe safety incidents, thereby ensuring the safe and stable operation of battery energy storage systems. To address this challenge, this study proposes a novel early ISC identification method for lithium-ion battery packs based on dynamic time warping (DTW) sequences and Gaussian mixture model (GMM) clustering. First, the median terminal voltage curve is derived from sorted terminal voltage measurements, serving as a reference representing the normal state of cells within the battery pack. Subsequently, sliding time windows are applied to extract subsequences of the median terminal voltage and individual cell terminal voltages. On this basis, the DTW sequences of each cell are computed and utilized as an indicator to characterize abnormal battery behavior, thereby amplifying the discrepancy between early ISC-affected cells and normal ones. Furthermore, an automatic early ISC fault detection model is developed using a GMM-based clustering algorithm to distinguish between normal and early ISC cells within the battery pack. Experimental validation and analysis under various early ISC fault scenarios with different severity levels demonstrate that the proposed method achieves accurate identification of early ISC cells when the short-circuit resistance is less than or equal to1000 Ω.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Internal short circuit
Dynamic time warping
Gaussian mixture model

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overall framework of the proposed method.
Voltage curves of all individual cells during cycles 3–6.
Voltage curves of all individual cells during cycles 7–10.
DTW sequences of all cells within the battery pack during cycles 3–6.
DTW sequences of all cells within the battery pack during cycles 7–10.
Fault detection results based on the DTW sequences.
Fault detection results based on the raw voltage curves.
DTW sequences of individual cells under different time window widths.
Raw voltage curves of all cells under different amplitudes of random noise interference in the voltage measurement of Cell 7.

row.entry:
Charging cut-off voltage
Discharging cut-off voltage

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Short-circuit resistors corresponding to different charge-discharge cycles.
Qualitative comparison between the proposed method and representative existing methods.
Quantitative comparison between the proposed method and representative existing methods.
Accuracy and runtime of the proposed method under different window widths.
Comparison of diagnostic accuracy based on raw voltage curves and DTW sequences when the voltage measurement of Cell 7 is contaminated by noise.

head.ce:title: Early detection of internal short circuit faults in lithium-ion battery packs using dynamic time warping and Gaussian mixture clustering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Grid Shanxi Electric Power Research Institute, Taiyuan, Shanxi Province 030001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Grid Shanxi Electric Power Research Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Grid Shanxi Electric Power Research Institute, Taiyuan 030001, Shanxi Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Harbin Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: No. 6 Qingnian Road, Yingze District, Taiyuan City, Shanxi Province 030001, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Internal short circuit (ISC) faults are a primary trigger for thermal runaway in energy storage lithium-ion battery systems. Timely detection of ISC faults at their early stage can effectively prevent severe safety incidents, thereby ensuring the safe and stable operation of battery energy storage systems. To address this challenge, this study proposes a novel early ISC identification method for lithium-ion battery packs based on dynamic time warping (DTW) sequences and Gaussian mixture model (GMM) clustering. First, the median terminal voltage curve is derived from sorted terminal voltage measurements, serving as a reference representing the normal state of cells within the battery pack. Subsequently, sliding time windows are applied to extract subsequences of the median terminal voltage and individual cell terminal voltages. On this basis, the DTW sequences of each cell are computed and utilized as an indicator to characterize abnormal battery behavior, thereby amplifying the discrepancy between early ISC-affected cells and normal ones. Furthermore, an automatic early ISC fault detection model is developed using a GMM-based clustering algorithm to distinguish between normal and early ISC cells within the battery pack. Experimental validation and analysis under various early ISC fault scenarios with different severity levels demonstrate that the proposed method achieves accurate identification of early ISC cells when the short-circuit resistance is less than or equal to1000 Ω.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: To address the dual challenges of the energy crisis and environmental pollution, the accelerated development of novel renewable and green energy sources has become a key direction for the future energy landscape [1]. However, the direct integration of renewable energy into the power grid can significantly compromise grid stability and operational safety. Consequently, the development of efficient and advanced energy storage technologies is a critical enabler for the large-scale deployment of renewable energy [2]. Among various energy storage options, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have emerged as a leading candidate due to their excellent cycling performance, high energy density, and environmentally friendly characteristics [3]. Nevertheless, as high-energy-density carriers, LIBs are prone to fire and explosion hazards under abusive conditions, which can trigger thermal runaway propagation within battery modules and, in severe cases, lead to fire spread and cascading failures across the entire energy storage system. The fire safety of LIBs for energy storage has thus become a pressing challenge that must be addressed to ensure the stable and sustainable development of the energy storage industry.

Internal short circuit (ISC) is a typical form of electrical abuse and one of the most common triggers of thermal runaway in LIBs. Broadly defined, an ISC refers to a discharge phenomenon accompanied by heat generation that occurs when the cathode and anode materials become electrically connected within the cell due to a potential difference [4]. ISCs represent a critical link in the chain of events leading to thermal runaway, regardless of whether the initiating abuse is mechanical, thermal, or electrical in nature. Additionally, the introduction of foreign particulate contaminants during electrode manufacturing, or improper electrode tab cutting that creates burrs, can also result in ISCs. Once an ISC occurs, the resulting short-circuit current generates Joule heat, which causes a rise in cell temperature. If local heat accumulation initiates a chain thermal runaway reaction, it may ultimately lead to catastrophic safety incidents such as fire or explosion, posing severe risks to both life and property. As the specific energy of LIB systems continues to increase—typically achieved by thickening electrode layers and thinning separators—the likelihood of ISCs correspondingly rises [5]. Therefore, the development of reliable ISC detection algorithms has become increasingly critical for ensuring the safety of LIBs.

Extensive evidence from related studies has demonstrated that the progression of an ISC prior to the onset of thermal runaway is characterized by a prolonged and gradual evolutionary process [6]. In the early stage of an ISC, the short-circuit resistance remains relatively high, resulting in low short-circuit current and limited heat generation. Consequently, no significant temperature rise is observed in the cell, and the ISC evolves very slowly, making the early stage markedly long in duration. In contrast, the terminal stage of an ISC is extremely brief, as the collapse of the separator can lead to an abrupt onset of thermal runaway. Since the early stage accounts for the vast majority of the ISC evolution timeline, and the transition to the terminal stage results in immediate thermal runaway, it is imperative that ISC detection and mitigation be realized during the early stage of its development.

The early detection of ISCs has become a prominent research focus in the field of LIB fault diagnosis. Researchers have proposed a variety of early ISC detection methods from different perspectives. Existing approaches can be broadly categorized into three types: methods based on inconsistency analysis, model-based methods, and methods based on self-discharge detection.

Inconsistency-based methods rely on the assumption of cell uniformity within a LIB pack, which posits that individual cells should exhibit similar behavior due to their production from the same batch and operation under comparable conditions. When an ISC occurs in a single cell, discrepancies arise in key parameters such as voltage, temperature, and state of charge (SOC) relative to the other cells in the pack. These discrepancies, referred to as consistency deviations, tend to increase following the onset of an ISC and can serve as the basis for ISC detection algorithms. For instance, Chang et al. [7] proposed a method based on the similarity of charging voltage curves, in which particle swarm optimization was employed to extract multiple consistency parameters. These parameters were then evaluated both longitudinally (across different cells) and laterally (over time), with the final fault alarm triggered based on the 3σ rule. Similarly, Lai et al. [8] quantified consistency deviations using correlation coefficients, whereby an increase in the deviation of a specific cell led to a reduction in its correlation with adjacent cells below a predefined threshold.

Model-based ISC detection methods aim to transform the ISC detection task into a problem of state estimation or parameter identification by leveraging physical LIB models. For example, Asakura et al. [9] proposed a method that identifies ISCs by comparing the model-predicted voltage with the measured voltage under constant current charging conditions. Feng et al. [10] employed an equivalent circuit model to identify parameters such as ohmic resistance and polarization resistance, showing that inputting the voltage and current profiles of normal and ISC cells leads to distinguishable parameter identification results, which can be used to detect ISCs. The accuracy of such methods is inherently dependent on the fidelity of the LIB model. However, in practical applications, model parameters are subject to real-time variations due to factors such as aging and ambient temperature, making the detection performance of these methods susceptible to uncertainties in model parameters [11].

Abnormal self-discharge beyond the normal range is one of the key indicators of ISCs. Accordingly, self-discharge-based detection methods aim to determine the presence of ISCs by monitoring deviations in self-discharge behavior. However, in the early stage of an ISC, the short-circuit resistance is relatively high, resulting in low short-circuit current. Thus, a central challenge for this class of methods lies in accurately measuring the small short-circuit current or amplifying its effect for reliable detection. For instance, Sazhin et al. [12] proposed a cell-level ISC detection method in which a constant voltage source is connected in parallel with the battery. Under equilibrium conditions, the current flowing through the battery corresponds to the short-circuit current, and high-precision current sensing is used to facilitate its measurement. Schmidt et al. [13] introduced a method based on monitoring the voltage relaxation curve following the application of discharge pulses; if abnormal self-discharge is present, the relaxation voltage exhibits a negative slope over an extended period. Considering practical implementation scenarios, Chang et al. [14] avoided direct estimation of short-circuit current and instead monitored changes in the voltage ranking of individual cells after the battery pack finishes charging. A persistent decline in a particular cell’s voltage ranking over time was interpreted as an indication of an ISC in that cell.

As demonstrated by the above analysis, existing research has made notable progress in the early detection of ISCs in LIBs. However, several limitations remain. First, most current methods rely on raw voltage data for ISC detection. In the early stage of an ISC, the voltage of the affected cell typically exhibits no significant deviation, making it difficult to distinguish from that of healthy cells. Consequently, methods based solely on raw voltage data are prone to misdiagnosis or missed detection. Second, the majority of existing approaches require manually predefined diagnostic thresholds during the detection process. The selection of such thresholds has a direct impact on detection accuracy: an overly high threshold may result in missed faults, while an overly low threshold may trigger false alarms. Determining an appropriate threshold generally demands extensive offline testing, which is time-consuming and impedes the online deployment of diagnostic algorithms.

To address the aforementioned challenges, this paper proposes an early ISC detection method for LIB packs based on dynamic time warping (DTW) sequences and Gaussian mixture model (GMM) clustering. First, at any given time, the terminal voltages of individual cells within the LIB pack are sorted to obtain a median voltage curve, which serves as a reference representing the normal state of cells in the pack. Next, within a sliding time window, the DTW distance between each cell’s terminal voltage curve and the median voltage curve is computed, yielding a DTW sequence for each cell. Subsequently, statistical features are extracted from these DTW sequences to characterize the battery states. These features are then input into an unsupervised clustering algorithm based on a GMM, enabling automatic identification of early ISC cells and normal cells within the LIB pack. Finally, the feasibility and accuracy of the proposed method are validated under early ISC faults of varying severity.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 provides a detailed description of the proposed diagnostic method; Section 3 presents the fault simulation process; Section 4 discusses the diagnostic results; finally, Section 5 concludes the paper.
As illustrated in Fig. 1, the proposed early ISC detection method for LIB packs consists of four main steps:

(1) At each time instant, the terminal voltages of all cells within the LIB pack are sorted in ascending order to obtain the median terminal voltage curve. In practical operation, only a small fraction of cells experience ISCs simultaneously, meaning that the majority of terminal voltages at any given time correspond to healthy cells. Furthermore, the median voltage is robust against extreme values, thus effectively representing the state of normal cells within the pack.

(2) Using a sliding time window, both the median terminal voltage curve and each individual cell’s terminal voltage curve are segmented into overlapping subsequences. The DTW distance is then calculated between corresponding subsequences of the median curve and each cell’s voltage curve.

(3) Based on the results from step (2), a DTW sequence is obtained for each cell. Two statistical features—the maximum and mean values of the DTW sequence—are extracted to characterize the state of each cell.
(4) The extracted statistical features are fed into a GMM clustering algorithm, which automatically identifies ISC cells and normal cells within the LIB pack based on the clustering outcomes.

From the aforementioned fault detection process, it can be seen that the proposed method transforms the raw voltage curves of individual cells into their corresponding DTW sequences. Compared to the original voltage sequences, DTW sequences are more sensitive to faults. Therefore, by converting the raw voltage data into DTW sequences, the method amplifies the differences between normal cells and ISC cells, facilitating fault detection and enabling more sensitive early ISC identification. The implementation of the proposed approach primarily involves two key techniques: DTW distance calculation and GMM clustering. The following sections provide a detailed description of these two modules.

As illustrated in Fig. 1, after obtaining the median terminal voltage curve based on the sorted terminal voltages, the median voltage curve and each individual cell’s terminal voltage curve are segmented into overlapping subsequences using a sliding time window. Subsequently, the DTW distance between the median voltage subsequences and the corresponding cell voltage subsequences is calculated to derive the DTW sequence for each cell. DTW distance efficiently aligns sequences of different lengths or with local temporal shifts by elastically matching them, thus addressing nonlinear distortions along the time axis [20,21]. This makes DTW particularly suitable for scenarios involving variable speed, local stretching, or compression. DTW has been widely applied across diverse fields including speech recognition, biosignal analysis, finance, and activity recognition. Additionally, DTW exhibits a certain degree of robustness to noise and amplitude variations.

We denote the subsequence of the median voltage curve as V
med and the subsequence of the terminal voltage curve of a given cell as V :
(1)
V
med
=
[
v
med
( 1 )
,
v
med
( 2 )
, … ,
v
med
( m )
]
(2)
V =
[
v
( 1 )
, v
( 2 )
, … , v
( l )
]
Where m and l represent the lengths of the median voltage curve subsequence and the individual cell terminal voltage subsequence, respectively. The distance matrix A is constructed based on the Euclidean distances between every pair of points from the two subsequences, as illustrated below:
(3)
A =
(
d
11

d
1 l
⋮ ⋱ ⋮
d
m 1
⋯
d
m l
)
where d
ij denotes the Euclidean distance between the i-th sample point v med(i) of the median voltage curve subsequence and the j-th sample point v(j) of the individual cell terminal voltage subsequence. The calculation formula is given as follows:
(4)
d
i j

‖

med
( i ) − v ( j )
‖
2
, 1 ≤ i ≤ m , 1 ≤ j ≤ l

At this point, the DTW distance D( V
med, V ) can be defined as the minimum cumulative distance between V
med and V along an optimal warping path W
best in the distance matrix A . The optimal warping path W
best is formally defined by the following equation:
(5)
{
W
best
=

Accordingly, the mathematical definition of DTW can be expressed as follows:
(6)
{
D

)

W

1 M

k = 1
M

k
}

k

( i , j )
k

In the above equation, W
k denotes the position of the warping path W in the distance matrix A . To ensure the uniqueness of the solution to the mathematical definition of DTW in Eq.(6), three constraints are imposed:

(1)Boundary Condition: The boundary points of the optimal warping path W
best are fixed, specifically W
1= (1,1) and W
k = (m,l).

(2)Monotonicity: This condition ensures the search direction for W
best. Specifically, for a given W
k = (i,j) and W
k+1= (i ∗,j ∗), it must hold that i*≥i, j*≥j.

(3)Continuity: The search for W
best can only proceed to adjacent points. Specifically, for a given W
k = (i,j) and W
k+1= (i ∗,j ∗), it must hold that i*≤i+1 and j*≤j+1.

Given the three constraints, the solution to Eq. (6) can be obtained using dynamic programming. The state transition equation for dynamic programming is as follows:
(7)
DTW ( i , j ) =
d
i j
+
min
[
DTW ( i − 1 , j − 1 ) , DTW ( i − 1 , j ) , DTW ( i , j − 1 )
]

Based on the aforementioned procedure, the DTW sequence for each individual cell can be obtained and subsequently used in place of the original voltage sequence for early ISC fault detection. Thereafter, the maximum and mean values of each cell's DTW sequence are calculated and employed as statistical features to characterize the cell’s operational state.

The DTW sequence effectively amplifies the differences between early ISC cells and normal cells, thereby facilitating early fault detection. In principle, after obtaining the DTW sequences for all cells within a LIB pack, early ISC cells can be identified by directly observing discrepancies among these sequences. Specifically, if the DTW sequence of a particular cell deviates significantly from those of the other cells, it may be classified as an early ISC cell. However, in practical applications, LIB packs typically consist of hundreds or even thousands of cells connected in series or parallel, resulting in a correspondingly large number of DTW sequences. Under such circumstances, manual identification based on visual inspection of sequence differences becomes highly impractical and lacks the necessary rigor, which may lead to misdiagnosis. Therefore, it is essential to develop an automated identification approach to ensure accurate and efficient detection of early ISC cells. To this end, this study extracts the maximum and mean values from each cell's DTW sequence as statistical features and employs a GMM-based clustering algorithm to automatically identify early ISC cells.

The GMM clustering is a probabilistic clustering approach that characterizes the spatial distribution and inherent properties of a dataset using a mixture of Gaussian distributions [22]. By iteratively optimizing the parameters of the GMM, the model is fitted to the data to achieve the best approximation [23]. Clustering is then performed based on the principle of maximum likelihood. This method not only provides a smooth approximation of the overall data distribution, but also captures the multimodal nature of the data through its individual components, thereby effectively accounting for correlations among data points. In an L-dimensional space, a GMM with K components is defined as follows:
(8)
{
p
(
x
| Θ
)
=
∑
k = 1
K
π
k
N
(
x
|
μ
k
,
Σ
k
)
N
(
x
|
μ
k
,
Σ
k
)
=
1
(
2 π
)
L / 2
|
Σ
k
|
1 / 2
exp
[
−
1 2
(
x −
μ
k
)
⊤
Σ
k
− 1
(
x −
μ
k
)
]
∑
k = 1
K
π
k
= 1 ,
π
k
≥ 0
where
p
(
x
| Θ
)
denotes the probability density function of the GMM, and
Θ =
{
π
k
,
μ
k
,
Σ
k
}
k = 1
K
represents the parameter set of the GMM.
N
(
x
|
μ
k
,

k
)
refers to the probability density function of the k-th Gaussian component, while
π
k
denotes its corresponding weight, also known as the mixing coefficient. The mean and covariance of the k-th component are represented by
μ
k
and
Σ
k
, respectively.

Clustering the dataset { x
1; x
2;…; x
N } using the GMM essentially involves performing maximum likelihood estimation of the GMM parameter set. The most commonly used approach for this purpose is the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm. The detailed steps of the GMM-based clustering procedure are outlined in Algorithm 1. As illustrated in Algorithm 1, the EM algorithm iteratively searches for the maximum likelihood estimates of the parameters by alternating between two steps after initialization: the Expectation step (E-step) and the Maximization step (M-step). In the E-step, the posterior probability
z ˆ
n , k
i
that sample x
n belongs to the k-th Gaussian component is computed based on the current model parameters. In the M-step, the model parameters are updated using the posterior probabilities obtained in the E-step for all data points and components. The E-step and M-step are repeated iteratively until the log-likelihood function converges or the number of iterations exceeds 100. Finally, the trained GMM is used to perform clustering on the dataset, and the classification results are obtained accordingly.

The number of components (i.e., the order) in a GMM is a critical parameter in the clustering process. Selecting the model order involves a trade-off between goodness of fit and model complexity. An model with too few components may fail to capture the underlying data structure, while an model with too many components can lead to overfitting. The Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) is a widely used method for determining the optimal number of GMM components. BIC evaluates models by considering both the log-likelihood and the model complexity, enabling a principled comparison of different models fitted to the same dataset. The BIC is defined as follows:
(8)
B I C = − 2
log
f
( Θ )
+ q ×
log
( n )
Where logf(·) denotes the log-likelihood function, n represents the number of observations, and q is the number of estimated parameters. A smaller BIC value indicates a better trade-off between model fit and complexity, and thus corresponds to a more favorable model. Algorithm 1
GMM-based clustering algorithm

To validate the proposed battery fault diagnosis method, it is necessary to controllably induce ISC faults in individual cells within the LIB pack. Currently, commonly used ISC induction methods can be categorized into three types: mechanical abuse, material implantation, and electrical abuse [15,16]. However, these conventional methods primarily simulate the late-stage ISC faults, often leading to rapid thermal runaway in the tested cells. Such approaches suffer from significant limitations, including poor controllability and low repeatability. In contrast, the external resistor shunting method enables precise simulation of early-stage ISC faults. This method involves connecting a controllable resistor network in parallel across the cell terminals, allowing flexible adjustment of resistance values to mimic different stages of ISC evolution [17–19]. Considering that the heat generation power of early ISCs is relatively low and that modern thermal management systems can effectively dissipate heat, the thermal effects can be approximately neglected. Therefore, this study adopts the external resistor shunting method as the experimental approach for simulating early ISCs. This method offers the following significant advantages: (1) precise control over the spatial and temporal characteristics of the short-circuit initiation; (2) the ability to fully replicate the dynamic evolution process of the short-circuit fault; and (3) excellent experimental controllability and repeatability, providing a reliable experimental foundation for in-depth investigation of early ISC characteristics.

The experimental platform architecture is illustrated in Fig. 2. The entire testing system operates within a temperature-controlled chamber maintaining an isothermal environment at 25 ± 0.5°C. A high-precision battery testing system, controlled by an upper computer, executes predefined charge–discharge protocols. Real-time data acquisition is conducted at a sampling frequency of 1 Hz via the TCP/IP communication protocol. Monitored parameters include individual cell terminal voltage, current, and surface temperature. The experimental subject is a series-connected battery pack composed of eight commercial ternary lithium-ion cells, with key performance parameters of each cell detailed in Table 1. The testing platform employs synchronized multi-parameter acquisition and closed-loop control to ensure the reliability and repeatability of experimental data.

The experiment employed a constant current charge–dynamic discharge composite protocol to perform cyclic testing on the LIB pack. A total of 10 complete charge–discharge cycles were conducted. To simulate the dynamic evolution characteristics of early ISCs, controllable resistor networks were connected in series with cells 4 and 8 starting from the fourth cycle. This network consisted of precision adjustable resistors and electromagnetic relays connected in series. By systematically adjusting the values of the parallel resistors (detailed in Table 2), the ISC progression from mild to severe was accurately simulated: smaller resistance values correspond to more severe short circuits and consequently higher short-circuit currents. This gradual parameter adjustment approach effectively enabled quantitative simulation of the ISC evolution process. Two battery packs with identical configurations underwent the same testing procedure; the data from the first pack served as the training set for subsequent method validation, while the second pack’s data was reserved as the test set. Each cell’s output during each charge–discharge cycle was treated as an individual sample. Thus, for each LIB pack, eight cells over ten cycles yielded a total of 80 samples, comprising 66 normal samples and 14 fault samples corresponding to early ISCs.

Figs. 3 and 4 respectively present the original terminal voltage curves of individual cells within the LIB pack during the 3rd to 10th cycles. As shown in Fig. 3(a), when no early ISC fault occurs in the LIB pack, the terminal voltage curves of all cells completely overlap, exhibiting excellent consistency. In contrast, Fig. 3(b) to Fig. 4(d) demonstrate that upon the occurrence of early ISC faults, the terminal voltage curves of the short-circuited cells 4 and 8 slightly deviate below those of the normal cells. This phenomenon arises because, during the LIB pack’s charge-discharge process, the short-circuit current continuously drains the energy of the affected cells, causing their terminal voltage and SOC to increase more slowly during charging compared to normal cells; conversely, during discharging, the terminal voltage and SOC of the short-circuited cells decrease faster than those of normal cells. It is important to note that the differences between the terminal voltage curves of cells 4 and 8 and those of the normal cells remain subtle, especially at the early ISC stage, due to the relatively high short-circuit resistance and correspondingly low short-circuit current, which are insufficient to induce significant electrothermal abnormalities. These observations indicate that relying solely on the original terminal voltage curves for early ISC detection imposes stringent requirements and severe challenges on the sensitivity and accuracy of fault detection algorithms. In other words, using the raw terminal voltage curves for early ISC diagnosis carries a considerable risk of misdiagnosis or missed detection. Therefore, the original terminal voltage curves are not the optimal diagnostic indicator for early ISC detection.

Based on the above analysis, to achieve accurate detection of early ISCs in LIB packs, this study employs the method proposed in Section 2.1 to obtain the DTW sequences of each individual cell.
Figs. 5 to 6 illustrate the DTW sequences of all cells in the LIB pack during cycles 3–10, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5(a), when no early ISC occurs within the LIB pack, the DTW sequence values of all cells remain low and exhibit good consistency across different cells without significant differences. Conversely, as depicted in Figs. 5(b) through 6(d), when early ISC faults arise, the DTW sequence values of the short-circuited cells 4 and 8 increase markedly, while the DTW values of the normal cells show minimal fluctuation compared to those in cycle 3. This results in a pronounced divergence between the DTW sequences of the short-circuited and normal cells. Furthermore, it can be observed that as the short-circuit resistance decreases, the DTW values of cells 4 and 8 progressively increase, thereby amplifying the difference relative to the normal cells’ DTW sequences. This analysis demonstrates that, compared to raw voltage differences, the DTW sequences highlight a more significant distinction between short-circuited and normal cells, confirming that DTW effectively amplifies these differences and facilitates early ISC detection. In other words, DTW sequences serve as a superior diagnostic indicator for early ISC detection compared to the original voltage curves.

After obtaining the DTW sequences for each cell, the maximum and mean values of each cell’s DTW sequence in each cycle were extracted as input features for automatic identification and differentiation of early ISC cells from normal cells within the LIB pack using a GMM clustering algorithm. For comparison, the maximum and mean values of the raw terminal voltage curves of each cell in each cycle were similarly extracted and input into the GMM-based clustering algorithm to perform early ISC detection. As described in Section 3, each cell’s output in each charge–discharge cycle was treated as an individual sample. Accordingly, for the LIB packs used in the experiments, a total of 80 samples were generated from 8 cells over 10 cycles, including 66 normal samples and 14 samples corresponding to early ISC faults.

The fault detection results based on the DTW sequences and the raw voltage curves are shown in
Figs. 7 and 8, respectively, where each data point represents a sample. Normal samples are indicated by circles, while faulty samples are represented by squares.

As shown in Fig. 7, the DTW-based method successfully detected 12 faulty samples, classifying the remaining samples as normal. By tracing the corresponding cell indices and charge/discharge cycles of each sample, it is found that the DTW-based method misclassified two faulty samples from the 4th cycle as normal. This misclassification occurred because the short-circuit resistance in the 4th cycle was relatively high (1100 Ω), resulting in a small short-circuit current that did not cause significant fluctuations in the DTW sequences of faulty cells 4 and 8. This can also be clearly observed in Fig. 5(b).

As shown in Fig. 8, the method based on raw voltage measurements identified only two faulty samples, misclassifying the majority of the faulty samples as normal. This indicates that the raw-voltage-based method exhibits very limited fault detection capability. A detailed analysis reveals that these two correctly detected faults occurred in the 10th cycle, where the short-circuit resistance was much lower (only 200 Ω), leading to pronounced abnormal fluctuations in the voltage profiles of the faulty cells.

In summary, the above analysis demonstrates that the proposed method can accurately and automatically identify and differentiate most early ISC cells from normal ones within the battery pack, validating the effectiveness and feasibility of the approach.

This subsection selects representative physics-based and data-driven approaches from existing studies as benchmark methods. The proposed method is qualitatively and quantitatively compared against these benchmarks. Specifically, the selected benchmarks include an equivalent circuit model combined with SoC correlation analysis (denoted as ECM-SoC) [8], a gradient boosting decision tree (GBDT) method [24], and an incremental capacity (IC)-based approach [25].

Firstly, a qualitative comparison is conducted between the baseline methods and the proposed method from three perspectives: the requirement for battery modeling, the need for diagnostic threshold setting, and the dependence on labeled data. The comparison results are summarized in Table 3. As Table 3 reveals, ECM-SoC methods require constructing an individual model for every cell in the pack. The accuracy of a lithium‑ion cell’s ECM is bounded by its parameters, which vary with temperature, ageing state, and other external factors during operation; thus, physics‑based approaches are inherently vulnerable to environmental disturbances. Moreover, practical battery packs typically comprise large series‑parallel assemblies, so modelling each cell and continuously updating its parameters imposes a prohibitive computational burden. The GBDT‑based approach depends critically on abundant, high‑quality labelled data. In real‑world energy‑storage systems, such data—especially those labelled with ISC faults—are exceedingly scarce because of cost and safety constraints, limiting the practicality of supervised machine‑learning methods like GBDT. Incremental‑capacity techniques must extract IC curves from constant‑current charging data—a process that demands low‑rate charging and therefore conflicts with today’s pursuit of high‑rate fast charging. In addition, both the ECM-SoC and IC‑based methods rely on manually predefined diagnostic thresholds. These thresholds directly determine diagnostic accuracy, yet in practice lack a clear scientific or statistical basis; obtaining reliable values typically requires extensive offline testing, which is time‑consuming and costly. Consequently, such thresholds diminish the overall practicality and generalization capability of the diagnostic approaches.

As shown in Table 3, the proposed method offers several advantages over existing approaches. First, it eliminates the need for battery modeling, thereby avoiding the computational burden associated with model construction and parameter updating. Second, it requires no manually predefined diagnostic thresholds, circumventing the challenge of threshold selection. Finally, the method leverages an unsupervised GMM clustering algorithm to automatically identify early-stage ISC cells within the battery pack, without the need for labeled data. In other words, the training process is conducted in an unsupervised manner using unlabeled datasets, making the required training data more accessible in real-world applications.

Next, the diagnostic performance of the baseline methods and the proposed method is quantitatively compared using accuracy as the evaluation metric. Specifically, the accuracy is defined as follows:
(9)
Acc =
N
AD
N
T
where Acc denotes the accuracy, while N AD and N T represent the number of accurately diagnosed samples and the total number of samples, respectively. A higher accuracy indicates better diagnostic performance. The hyperparameter and threshold settings used for implementing the baseline methods were selected according to the references in which each method was originally proposed, ensuring their optimal performance.

The comparison results of diagnostic accuracy are presented in Table 4. As shown, the proposed method achieves an accuracy of 97.50 %, which exceeds that of all three baseline methods by at least 3.75 %. The accuracy of all three baseline methods falls below 94 %, primarily because they rely on raw voltage sequences or incremental capacity curves for fault diagnosis. As previously discussed, during the early stage of ISCs, the raw voltage curves of lithium-ion batteries typically exhibit no pronounced anomalies, which in turn leads to minimal changes in the corresponding incremental capacity curves. In summary, compared to existing methods, the proposed approach demonstrates superior overall diagnostic performance and offers greater practical applicability.

As described in Section 2, the proposed method first employs a sliding time window to convert the raw voltage sequences of individual cells within the battery pack into DTW sequences, thereby amplifying the differences between short-circuited and normal cells. Two features are then extracted from the DTW sequences to characterize the battery condition. Finally, early-stage ISC faults are automatically identified based on GMM clustering. Throughout the implementation of the method, two parameters need to be predefined: the number of components (i.e., the order) in the GMM and the width of the sliding time window. Among them, as shown in Eq.(8), the number of components in the GMM is determined during offline training using the Bayesian information criterion and remains fixed during the fault detection process. In other words, this parameter does not require manual tuning. Therefore, the most critical hyperparameter that needs to be manually set in the proposed method is the width of the sliding time window. The impact of this hyperparameter on the diagnostic performance of the proposed method is discussed in the following subsection.

Fig. 9 takes a specific cycle as an example to illustrate the DTW sequences of all individual cells in the battery pack under different sliding time window widths. A close examination of Fig. 9 reveals that as the window width increases, the DTW sequence values of all cells gradually increase. However, it is important to note that the difference in DTW sequences between the short-circuited cells (Cells 4 and 8) and the normal cells does not change significantly with increasing window width. In other words, the relative differences in DTW sequences among cells do not increase or decrease markedly as the window width increases. This indicates that the window width does not affect the fault detection accuracy of the proposed method.

To validate this conclusion, the proposed method was executed under different window widths, and both the diagnostic accuracy and the required computation time were observed. The detailed results are presented in Table 5. A careful examination of Table 5 shows that as the window width increases, the diagnostic accuracy of the proposed method remains unchanged, whereas the computation time increases. The increase in computation time is attributed to the higher computational load involved in calculating DTW distances using dynamic programming as the window width grows.

Based on the above analysis, the most influential hyperparameter in the proposed method is the width of the sliding time window used in the DTW sequence computation. As this parameter varies, the diagnostic accuracy of the method remains unchanged; however, the runtime increases with larger window widths. In light of this finding, the window width is set to 10 in this study to achieve faster detection speed.

During the actual operation of lithium-ion battery packs, voltage sensors inevitably exhibit inherent measurement errors. To analyze the sensitivity of the proposed method to such errors, a charge-discharge cycle with a short-circuit resistance of 500 Ω is taken as an example. Random noise of varying amplitudes is injected into the voltage measurements of Cell 7 to simulate measurement errors of different magnitudes. Specifically, the injected random noise has a mean of zero and standard deviations of 0.001 V, 0.002 V, 0.02 V, and 0.2 V, respectively. Fig. 10 shows the raw voltage curves of all cells in the battery pack when the voltage measurement of Cell 7 is affected by measurement errors of different magnitudes. As observed from Fig. 10, the distortion in Cell 7’s voltage measurement increases with the noise standard deviation. When the standard deviation reaches 0.02 V, the voltage measurement becomes severely distorted, and at 0.2 V, it becomes completely corrupted. Under these conditions, the diagnostic accuracy based on raw voltage measurements and that based on DTW sequences are compared. The results are summarized in Table 6. From Table 6, it can be observed that due to the misclassification of Cell 7 as a short-circuited cell, the accuracy of the method based on raw voltage sequences starts to decline even when the noise standard deviation is as low as 0.001 V. In contrast, the DTW-based method maintains stable diagnostic accuracy when the standard deviation is 0.002 V and 0.02 V, indicating that Cell 7 is not misclassified in these cases. Only when the standard deviation increases to 0.2 V—resulting in completely distorted voltage measurements—is Cell 7 misclassified as a short-circuited cell, leading to a drop in diagnostic accuracy.

From the above analysis, it can be seen that the diagnostic results based on raw voltage measurements are highly sensitive to measurement errors and are prone to misdiagnosis due to such errors. In contrast, the diagnostic results based on DTW sequences demonstrate stronger noise robustness and are less sensitive to noise. This is because the DTW sequences of each cell are calculated based on the median terminal voltage curves, and the median is less affected by extreme maximum or minimum values, thereby providing a certain degree of noise immunity. In summary, the DTW sequence–based method proposed in this paper exhibits a notable ability to resist noise interference. It should be noted that in practical applications, lithium-ion battery voltages are usually collected using high-precision integrated circuits, making severe or complete voltage distortion rare. Therefore, the proposed method has good practicality and high engineering application value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of short-circuit resistance magnitude on diagnostic results
ce:section.ce:para: The fault detection results of the proposed method are shown in Fig. 7. By tracing back the charge-discharge cycles and cell numbers corresponding to each sample in Fig. 7, it can be seen that the proposed method can accurately detect short-circuited cells when the short-circuit resistance is less than or equal to 1050 Ω. However, when the short-circuit resistance is 1100 Ω, the proposed method misclassifies the short-circuited cell as normal, resulting in a missed diagnosis. This result indicates that the detection capability of the proposed method has an upper limit: when the short-circuit resistance exceeds a certain value, the method cannot detect the internal short circuit fault. This is because the short-circuit resistance is too high at that point, leading to a very small short-circuit current and thus only slight abnormal changes in the battery voltage, which in turn causes the abnormal variations in the DTW sequence to be insufficiently pronounced, as shown in Fig. 5(b).

It should be noted that the method proposed in this paper can still detect short-circuited cells with a short-circuit resistance of 1050 Ω. Calculating the short-circuit current based on the rated voltage, the corresponding short-circuit current is approximately 0.00083 C. In other words, even when the short-circuit resistance is as high as 1050 Ω and the short-circuit current is as low as 0.00083 C, the proposed method can still accurately detect the short-circuited cells. A short-circuit current of 0.00083 C is already an extremely small value, corresponding to the early stage of an internal short circuit in lithium-ion batteries. Therefore, although the fault detection capability of the proposed method has an upper limit, this does not diminish its effectiveness and feasibility.

The implementation of the proposed method involves two core modules executed sequentially: the calculation of DTW sequences and GMM clustering. Therefore, the computational complexity of this method arises from these two core algorithms. It should be noted that compared to complex deep learning algorithms such as deep neural networks and convolutional neural networks, the computational complexity of the proposed method is significantly lower. Moreover, since the method does not require battery modeling, it avoids the computational burden associated with battery modeling and parameter updating. In applications with ample computing resources, such as large-scale energy storage power stations, the proposed method can be deployed directly in practical battery management systems. For scenarios with limited computational resources, such as electric vehicles, the method can be implemented on cloud platforms, with diagnostic results transmitted in real time to the vehicle, enabling automatic identification and warning of early ISC faults within lithium-ion battery packs. In resource-constrained environments like electric vehicles, intelligent BMS based on vehicle-cloud collaboration has been increasingly researched and developed; such BMSs are known for their powerful computing resources and real-time analysis capabilities. In summary, the proposed method has promising prospects for practical deployment.

This study proposes an unsupervised method for the automatic identification of early ISC faults in LIB packs for energy storage applications, leveraging DTW sequences and GMM clustering. Experimental simulations involving ISCs of varying severity were conducted to validate the proposed method, yielding the following key findings and conclusions:

(1) The proposed method enables accurate detection and identification of early ISCs in LIB packs without requiring battery modeling or predefined diagnostic thresholds. This effectively circumvents two major challenges in practical diagnosis: the difficulty of constructing accurate battery models and the uncertainty in threshold selection.

(2) The method extracts DTW sequences from raw terminal voltage measurements of individual cells and uses them as indicators of cell health states. Experimental results demonstrate that the DTW-based differences between faulty and healthy cells are significantly more pronounced than direct voltage differences. This enhanced sensitivity facilitates the accurate distinction of ISC cells from normal cells during early fault stages.

(3) The proposed method employs a GMM-based clustering algorithm for fault identification. As GMM is an unsupervised learning technique, the method requires no labeled training data, which greatly improves its applicability in real-world scenarios. Experimental results show that the method accurately distinguishes ISC cells from healthy ones when the short-circuit resistance is less than or equal to 1000 Ω.

Due to constraints in experimental cost and time, validation in this work was conducted on cylindrical ternary LIB packs. Future research will focus on extending the applicability of the proposed method to large-scale LIB systems with different chemistries and formats, thereby further verifying its effectiveness and generalizability in practical energy storage applications.

Ke Fu: Writing – review & editing. Xiaogang Du: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Zhuang Liu: Funding acquisition. Qi Zuo: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Meng Zhang: Visualization, Project administration, Data curation. Chao Lyu: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the project from State Grid Shanxi Electric Power Company [Grant No. 520530240027].
